serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed white rice as typically served in a Singaporean Chinese meal, approximately 180 g cooked weight.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked, unenriched, without salt
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods / SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked plain white rice (unenriched, no salt) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g cooked weight. This is consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for plain steamed white rice (approximately 130 kcal per 100 g cooked), confirming alignment with regional data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 for white rice (boiled/steamed) is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.76) / 100 ≈ 36.5, rounded to 36.4. Serving carbohydrate = 28.2 g per 100 g × 180 g serving = 50.76 g.

hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed white rice contains approximately 1 mg sodium per 100 g and poses negligible sodium load for hypertensive individuals; portion control remains advisable to limit overall caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice has a high GI of 72 and delivers a glycemic load of approximately 36 per standard bowl, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider substituting with brown rice or basmati rice, and pair with protein and vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain steamed white rice is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and sodium (1 mg per 100 g), making it a heart-neutral staple when consumed in moderate portions as part of a balanced diet; excessive refined carbohydrate intake may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia if portions are large.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white rice (白饭, Bai Fan) is the foundational staple grain of Chinese and broader Southeast Asian cuisine, including Singapore, where it is served at virtually every meal as an accompaniment to meat, fish, vegetable, and tofu dishes. It is prepared by washing polished long-grain or medium-grain white rice and cooking it with water by steaming or absorption boiling until the grains are tender, separate, and slightly sticky. The texture is soft and fluffy with a mild, neutral flavour that absorbs the sauces and seasonings of accompanying dishes. Nutritionally, cooked white rice provides approximately 130 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient (28.2 g per 100 g), very low fat (0.3 g), and modest protein (2.7 g). It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. Its high glycemic index (GI 72) means it causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content is negligible (1 mg per 100 g), making it suitable for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore hawker culture, a standard bowl of white rice (约 180 g) is priced between SGD 1.00 and SGD 1.80 and is a universal side dish across Chinese, Malay, and Indian meal contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of white rice?
Is white rice good for diabetics?
What is the GI of white rice?
How much carbohydrate in steamed rice?
Is white rice suitable for hypertension?
What is 白饭 in English?
How much sodium in plain rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) for plain cooked white rice, which is an exact match to this food. GI value of 72 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. HPB Singapore Foodo data for plain steamed white rice is consistent with these values. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean Chinese dish are present.
notes: Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked white rice with no added fats. Sugar (sugar_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — plain cooked white rice contains no free sugars. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; naturally zero as white rice contains no ascorbic acid. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is 0.0; naturally zero in plain white rice. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0; naturally zero as this is a plant-based staple grain. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is 0.0; naturally zero in plain white rice. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for unenriched cooked white rice. Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0; naturally zero as rice is a plant food. Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g = 0.1) and polyunsaturated fat (polyunsaturated_fat_g = 0.1) are reported per USDA FoodData Central. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrients are reported from USDA FoodData Central. Serving size of 180 g represents a standard single bowl as served in Singapore hawker centres. GI of 72 represents white rice (boiled/steamed) per University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 50.76) / 100 = 36.5 (rounded to 36.4), where serving carbohydrate = 28.2 g/100 g × 180 g = 50.76 g. Price range SGD 1.00–1.80 preserved from field survey data input data for contextual reference only; not used as a nutritional source.
